title: The mouth of the kitchen
summary: Forty minutes before lunch service, a head chef tells his sous-chef something he has hidden for eight months — and gives her a new role she did not ask for.

body.speaker: Lien
body.text: Chef, the lunch service starts in forty minutes. The new soup — I made it the way you taught me. Will you taste it?

body.speaker: Chef Quan
body.text: Lien. We have not talked about this. I will not taste it. Not because I am being difficult. Because I cannot.

body.speaker: Lien
body.text: Cannot?

body.speaker: Chef Quan
body.text: I lost my sense of taste eight months ago. After my illness. It did not come back.

body.speaker: Lien
body.text: Eight months ago. You have been cooking for eight months without tasting.

body.speaker: Chef Quan
body.text: Yes. With your help, though you did not know you were helping.

body.speaker: Lien
body.text: Why did you not tell me?

body.speaker: Chef Quan
body.text: Because if I had told you, you would have started cooking for me. I needed you to keep cooking for the customer. The two are different.

body.speaker: Lien
body.text: Chef. How have you been making decisions?

body.speaker: Chef Quan
body.text: From memory. I know what salt should look like in a pot. I know what fish smells like at three minutes versus four. I know what the colour of brown butter is when it is right and when it is wrong. I cook by sight, smell, sound, and memory. Taste was always only one of the five.

body.speaker: Lien
body.text: But the customers.

body.speaker: Chef Quan
body.text: Have not complained. Look at the reviews. They are the same as before. Slightly better, in fact, because I now plate more carefully.

body.speaker: Lien
body.text: Why are you telling me now?

body.speaker: Chef Quan
body.text: Because I will need you to help me, soon, more honestly. I want you to taste every dish before it goes out. Not as a check on my memory. As a check on the dish itself. You will be the mouth of the kitchen.

body.speaker: Lien
body.text: Chef, that is a lot of responsibility.

body.speaker: Chef Quan
body.text: It is. You are ready. I have watched you for three years.

body.speaker: Lien
body.text: What if I disagree with you about a dish?

body.speaker: Chef Quan
body.text: Then you will be right, and I will change the dish. I have been wrong before. I am sixty-three. I will be wrong again.

body.speaker: Lien
body.text: Will you eat it yourself, at home?

body.speaker: Chef Quan
body.text: No. I do not eat much anymore. Food is now mostly something I make for other people. That is a smaller life than I used to have. But it is still a life. And I want this kitchen to keep being a place where people eat well.

body.speaker: Lien
body.text: Yes, Chef.

grammarNotes.title: Third conditional: 'If X had…, Y would have…'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu điều kiện loại 3 (if + past perfect, would + have + V3) nói về một việc đáng lẽ xảy ra trong quá khứ nhưng không xảy ra. Trong bài: 'If I had told you, you would have started cooking for me.' (Nếu tôi đã nói với cô, cô đã bắt đầu nấu cho tôi.) Ông đã không nói, nên cô đã không làm vậy.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Third conditional (if + past perfect, would + have + past participle) describes a past event that did not happen. From the dialogue: 'If I had told you, you would have started cooking for me.' He did not tell her, so she did not.

blanks.explanation: He will not taste it — because he cannot.
He lost taste eight months ago.
After his illness — taste did not come back.
He needed her to keep cooking for the customer, not for him.
He knows what salt should look like in a pot.
Brown butter — the colour gives the right point.
Look at the reviews — same as before.
He will need her to help him more honestly from now on.
She will be the mouth of the kitchen — tasting for everyone.
He has watched her for three years and judges her ready.
He is sixty-three — old enough to expect future wrongness.
A smaller life than he used to have — but still a life.
He wants the kitchen to keep being a place where people eat well.

translationVi:
Liên: Bếp trưởng, ca trưa bắt đầu trong bốn mươi phút nữa. Món súp mới — em làm theo cách thầy dạy. Thầy nếm thử nhé?
Bếp trưởng Quân: Liên. Chúng ta chưa nói chuyện này. Tôi sẽ không nếm. Không phải vì tôi khó tính. Vì tôi không thể.
Liên: Không thể?
Bếp trưởng Quân: Tôi mất vị giác tám tháng trước. Sau khi tôi bị bệnh. Nó không trở lại.
Liên: Tám tháng trước. Thầy nấu suốt tám tháng mà không nếm.
Bếp trưởng Quân: Đúng. Có em giúp, dù em không biết là em đang giúp.
Liên: Sao thầy không nói cho em?
Bếp trưởng Quân: Vì nếu tôi đã nói, em sẽ bắt đầu nấu cho tôi. Tôi cần em tiếp tục nấu cho khách. Hai cái khác nhau.
Liên: Vậy thầy ra quyết định bằng cách nào?
Bếp trưởng Quân: Bằng trí nhớ. Tôi biết muối trong nồi trông thế nào là đúng. Tôi biết cá ngửi như thế nào ở phút thứ ba so với phút thứ tư. Tôi biết màu của bơ rán đến vàng nâu lúc nào đúng, lúc nào sai. Tôi nấu bằng mắt, mùi, âm thanh, và trí nhớ. Vị giác luôn chỉ là một trong năm.
Liên: Còn khách thì sao ạ?
Bếp trưởng Quân: Khách không phàn nàn. Em nhìn các đánh giá đi. Cũng như trước. Có khi còn hơi tốt hơn, vì tôi giờ bày đĩa kỹ hơn.
Liên: Sao thầy nói cho em bây giờ?
Bếp trưởng Quân: Vì tôi sẽ cần em giúp tôi, sắp tới, một cách thành thật hơn. Tôi muốn em nếm từng món trước khi món ra. Không phải để kiểm tra trí nhớ tôi. Mà để kiểm tra chính món ăn. Em sẽ là cái 'miệng' của căn bếp.
Liên: Thầy, đó là một trách nhiệm lớn.
Bếp trưởng Quân: Đúng. Em đã sẵn sàng. Tôi đã quan sát em ba năm.
Liên: Lỡ em không đồng ý với thầy về một món thì sao?
Bếp trưởng Quân: Thì em sẽ đúng, và tôi sẽ đổi món. Tôi đã sai trước đây. Tôi sáu mươi ba tuổi. Tôi sẽ còn sai.
Liên: Ở nhà thầy có ăn không ạ?
Bếp trưởng Quân: Không. Tôi không ăn nhiều nữa. Thức ăn giờ chủ yếu là cái tôi làm cho người khác. Đó là một cuộc đời nhỏ hơn cuộc đời tôi từng có. Nhưng vẫn là một cuộc đời. Và tôi muốn căn bếp này tiếp tục là nơi người ta ăn ngon.
Liên: Vâng, thưa thầy.

questions.prompt: What does Lien ask Chef Quan at the start?
options: Will he taste the new soup before lunch service?
Will he hire a new sous-chef?
He cannot — he lost his sense of taste eight months ago, after an illness
questions.explanation: 'I lost my sense of taste eight months ago. After my illness.'
questions.hint: Look at Chef Quan's second turn.

questions.prompt: Why didn't he tell Lien earlier?
options: If he had told her, she would have started cooking for him; he needed her to keep cooking for the customer
questions.explanation: 'If I had told you, you would have started cooking for me.'
questions.hint: Look at his reply to 'Why did you not tell me?'

questions.prompt: How has he been making decisions without taste?
options: From memory — sight, smell, sound, and memory; taste was only one of the five senses
questions.explanation: He lists the four senses he still uses.
questions.hint: Look at his long answer about decisions.

questions.prompt: What does he know about salt, fish, and butter from memory?
options: What salt should look like in a pot; how fish smells at three vs four minutes; the colour of brown butter when right and when wrong
questions.explanation: Each is listed in his long turn.

questions.prompt: What has happened to customer reviews in eight months?
options: They are the same as before — slightly better, in fact, because he now plates more carefully
questions.explanation: 'They are the same as before. Slightly better, in fact, because I now plate more carefully.'
questions.hint: Look at his reply about customers.

questions.prompt: Why is he telling Lien now?
options: He will need her to taste every dish before it goes out — as a check on the dish itself, not on his memory
questions.explanation: 'I want you to taste every dish before it goes out.'
questions.hint: Look at his reply to 'Why now?'

questions.prompt: What does he call this new role?
questions.explanation: 'You will be the mouth of the kitchen.'
questions.hint: Look at his definition of the new role.

questions.prompt: How long has he watched her, and what is his judgement?
options: Ten years — she is too senior
questions.explanation: 'You are ready. I have watched you for three years.'
questions.hint: Look at his reply to 'a lot of responsibility'.

questions.prompt: What will he do if Lien disagrees with him about a dish?
options: Concede — 'you will be right, and I will change the dish'
questions.explanation: 'You will be right, and I will change the dish.'
questions.hint: Look at his reply to 'What if I disagree?'

questions.prompt: Why does he say he will be wrong again?
options: He is sixty-three; he has been wrong before; this is normal
questions.explanation: 'I have been wrong before. I am sixty-three. I will be wrong again.'

questions.prompt: Does Chef Quan eat much at home?
options: No — food is now mostly something he makes for other people
questions.explanation: 'No. I do not eat much anymore. Food is now mostly something I make for other people.'
questions.hint: Look at his reply to 'Will you eat it yourself, at home?'

questions.prompt: How does he describe his life now?
options: A smaller life than he used to have — but still a life — and he wants the kitchen to keep being a place where people eat well
questions.explanation: 'That is a smaller life than I used to have. But it is still a life.'

questions.prompt: Which paraphrase is closer to the dialogue's central move?
options: A boss reveals a weakness and offloads work.
An older chef who can no longer taste hands over the sensory authority of the kitchen to a younger colleague he has been quietly preparing, and asks for honest disagreement.
A mentor hides a secret to protect his image.
A junior employee threatens her boss.
questions.explanation: He explains the strategy and explicitly asks her to disagree when she should.
questions.hint: Look at the offered role and the way disagreements are framed.

questions.prompt: What is the tone of the conversation?
options: Honest and dignified — neither performs the loss; the focus is on the next thing the kitchen needs
questions.explanation: He names the loss, sets a practical plan, ends with Lien's simple acceptance.
questions.hint: Notice how each speaker handles the news and the request.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Chef Quan says, 'I cook by sight, smell, sound, and memory. Taste was always only one of the five.' Think of a skill of yours that depends on multiple senses or kinds of attention. If you lost one of them, which of the others would have to carry the weight — and would the work still be the same work?
